coredata.dc:title: Performance-enhanced cellulose ternary composite in removing toxic lead ion pollutants: Atom-level interfacial structures and pseudo-dynamic trapping process
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Hong-Xue
Wang, Chen
Zheng, Wen-Xiu
Guo, Yuan-Ru
Pan, Qing-Jiang
coredata.dc:description: It is appealing but challenging to modulate interfacial structures and reactivity of biomass composites at the atomic level, due to its importance in guiding experimental synthesis and developing application. In this work, the ternary composite, carbon dots-Mg(OH)2-cellulose (CMCel), was examined by experimental characterizations (XRD, FT-IR, SEM, TEM, and XPS) and theoretical computations, along with its immobilization of heavy metal ion (HMI) Pb(II) from wastewater. Using a facile hydrothermal method, the composite material was prepared. It gives a high removal capacity up to 1623.0 mg g–1 towards Pb(II). Moreover, the removal rate maintains almost 99.5 % even after 10-cycle operation. The outstanding performance is attributed to synergetic effects of chemically coupled components in the composite. Specifically, interfacial hydrogen bonds are unraveled by density functional theory calculations and experimental characterizations. The HMI removal is well delineated via a pseudo-dynamic process. The in-depth understanding of interfacial behaviors of CMCel is anticipated to advance the design of novel water treatment agents and improve their removal performance towards HMIs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cellulose nanofiber
Magnesium hydroxide
Interfacial coupling
Removal mechanism of lead ion
DFT calculation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Natural Science Foundations of China ( 22276046 and U2167219 ); and the fund of the reformation and development of local universities supported by Central Government (2021) is appreciated. QJP is grateful to Dr. Dimitri Laikov for providing us with the Priroda code.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR spectrum (b) of CMCel, together with its SEM (c, d) and TEM (e, f) images.
Calculated formation reaction energies (eV) of binary (a) and ternary (b) composites starting from their precursors.
Optimized structures of various isomers of the tertiary composite CMCel_n (n = 1∼4), along with those of their precursors.
Energy decomposition analyses of interfacial couplings of CMCel_n (n = 1∼4) (a), along with the contribution (%) of each negative term (b).
Density of states of CMCel_1, along with diagrams of some selected orbitals.
XRD patterns of cellulose-Pb3(CO3)2(OH)2 (a), SEM images (b, c), EDS mapping (d), along with XPS spectra of the survey (e) and Pb 4f (f).

The adsorption kinetic curve while CMCel removes Pb2+ ions (a) and its pseudo-first/second-order fittings (b), the adsorption isotherm (c) and its Langmuir (d) and Freundlich fittings (e), as well as the durability test (f), where the 20 mg·L–1 Pb2+ solution was used for the durability test and the 500 mg·L–1 for other cases.
Adsorption efficiency of Pb2+ by CMCel against interfering cations (a) and in different initial pH solutions (b).
The proposed pseudo-dynamic process that while the ternary composite removes lead ion.
QTAIM data a (au.) at interfacial BCPs of CMCel_1, together with the ellipticity ε and interaction energy Eint (eV).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: QTAIM data include the electron density ρ(r), Laplacian ∇2ρ(r), and energy density H(r).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: All the BCPs chosen were located at the interface. O and H atoms in carbon dots were marked with a subscript of “C”, and those of magnesium oxide with a subscript of “M”. And no labeling was used for O and H atoms of cellulose.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: H(r) is the sum of the potential V(r) and kinetic G(r) energy densities.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Eint is the interaction energy and equal to half of V(r).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Calculated reaction energies (eV) while the ternary composite gathers lead nitrate and the cellulose immobilizes precipitates.

head.ce:title: Performance-enhanced cellulose ternary composite in removing toxic lead ion pollutants: Atom-level interfacial structures and pseudo-dynamic trapping process

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Ministry of Education), College of Material Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Ministry of Education), College of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bKey Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Ministry of Education), College of Material Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is appealing but challenging to modulate interfacial structures and reactivity of biomass composites at the atomic level, due to its importance in guiding experimental synthesis and developing application. In this work, the ternary composite, carbon dots-Mg(OH)2-cellulose (CMCel), was examined by experimental characterizations (XRD, FT-IR, SEM, TEM, and XPS) and theoretical computations, along with its immobilization of heavy metal ion (HMI) Pb(II) from wastewater. Using a facile hydrothermal method, the composite material was prepared. It gives a high removal capacity up to 1623.0 mg g–1 towards Pb(II). Moreover, the removal rate maintains almost 99.5 % even after 10-cycle operation. The outstanding performance is attributed to synergetic effects of chemically coupled components in the composite. Specifically, interfacial hydrogen bonds are unraveled by density functional theory calculations and experimental characterizations. The HMI removal is well delineated via a pseudo-dynamic process. The in-depth understanding of interfacial behaviors of CMCel is anticipated to advance the design of novel water treatment agents and improve their removal performance towards HMIs.

Ternary biobased composite has been prepared by a facile method. The synergistic chemical coupling of Mg(OH)2 with cellulose and CDs enables their composite to remove heavy metal lead ion rapidly and completely. Electrostatically-driven and thermodynamically-spontaneous HMI immobilization process is proposed by a combination of experimental characterizations and DFT calculations.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid global population growth and industrialization, the discharge of industrial, agricultural, and domestic wastewater has significantly increased, leading to severe water pollution. This issue poses urgent environmental challenges globally, impacting both human well-beings and ecosystems [1–6]. Among the common water pollutants are heavy metal ions (HMIs), originating mainly from industrial wastewater, mining, and smelting activities. Examples of HMIs include lead, mercury, cadmium, chromium and other non-biodegradable elements. They raise concerns due to their toxicity and persistence. Accumulation of HMIs in water bodies poses threats to aquatic organisms and human health, with lead being particularly hazardous when ingested or inhaled.

To address these concerns, stringent regulations on permissible lead concentrations in various products and environments have been implemented [7]. For instance, the World Health Organization (WHO) has established a provisional tolerable weekly intake (PTWI) for lead at 25 micrograms per kilogram of body weight per week. In the context of drinking water, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set an action level of 15 parts per billion (ppb) for lead in public water systems. Exceeding this level triggers mandatory actions by water providers to mitigate lead exposure. Similarly, China has established a stringent standard in its drinking water hygiene regulations, stipulating a permissible limit of 0.01 mg/L for lead. These rigorous standards underscore the commitment to safeguarding the health of populations and preventing adverse effects linked to lead contamination in water sources [8,9].

Efforts to mitigate water pollution caused by HMIs have led to extensive research on removal methods. Of them, the precipitation and immobilization emerge as a commonly used, cost-effective one. It involves the addition of precipitants or precipitating agents. By reacting with HMIs, the resultant solid precipitates can be separated and treated for efficient heavy metal removal and recovery [10–17]. In recent years, various effective materials have been used for precipitation and immobilization of heavy metals, such as iron oxide [18], sulfides [19,20], silica gel [21], activated carbon [22,23], carbon dots (CDs) [24,25], magnesium hydroxide (Mg(OH)2), and cellulose [26]. Currently, researchers have devoted to developing new composite materials that are low-cost, effective, and capable of removing heavy metals with greater efficiency; and optimization of precipitation and immobilization processes and sustainability is also considered. Therefore, the demand for economically viable, efficient, and user-friendly methods for heavy metal removal remains ongoing.

In the field of water treatment, carbon dots and cellulose are both widely recognized for their environmentally friendly and safe properties [27–29]. Carbon dots are increasingly popular due to their high specific surface area and porosity, which make them effective in capturing organic pollutants like pesticides, chemicals, drugs, and organic solvents. They also possess antibacterial and antimicrobial properties according to recent investigation [30]. Furthermore, functionalized carbon dots possess ion exchange capability and selective adsorption characteristics [30]. This means they can effectively adsorb heavy metal pollutants appearing in water, such as lead, mercury, and cadmium. This makes them a valuable candidate for the removal of heavy metal contaminants from water sources [31].

On the other hand, cellulose, a natural compound derived from various plants, is known for its renewable nature and biocompatibility. It has excellent adsorption and filtration capabilities, effectively removing impurities such as suspended solids, color, and odor from water [32,33]. Additionally, cellulose itself exhibits powerful antibacterial properties [34,35]. One of the advantages of using cellulose in water treatment is its cost-effectiveness. Extracting cellulose from plants is relatively inexpensive compared to other reagents used for water treatment. This offers cellulose composite materials a promising option as the primary component in water treatment agents. The interface behaviors of cellulose composite materials have attracted a lot attention. This is because the interfacial properties play a crucial role in guiding the synthesis of biomaterial composites with desired performance. Understanding and optimizing the interface behavior of composites, especially at the atomic level, can lead to the development of highly efficient and effective water treatment agents [36,37].

The 2D form of Mg(OH)2 is a widely available metal hydroxide that can effectively remove various types of HMIs from wastewater. However, managing large surface areas and highly active sites of magnesium hydroxide is a challenging task. So, it is necessary to develop and improve synthetic methods to address these issues. Although some studies on Mg(OH)2/CDs or cellulose composites in the field of wastewater treatment have been reported [26], little is known about the preparation and assembly mechanism of components of carbon dots, magnesium hydroxide, and cellulose in a ternary composite [38]. By leveraging the strengths of these three components and clarifying dynamic process that their compose remove HMIs, especially the exceptional adsorption capacity and ion exchange performance, we anticipate to be able to achieve more effective results in the future applications.

Aim to clearly picture the immobilization of lead ion, a ternary composite CDs/Mg(OH)2/Cellulose (CMCel) was prepared using a facile method firstly. It exhibits a multilayer lamellar array structure with a large specific surface area. CDs were synthesized from natural cellulose products. Then, the interfacial behaviors of CDs, Mg(OH)2 and cellulose as well as their composite to HMI was analyzed through both experimental characterizations and density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

The crystallinity of CMCel samples was analyzed by using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Nippon Science Company). In the operation, Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) was applied over 2θ from 10° to 80° with the scanning speed of 4°/min. The morphology was characterized by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, model S-4800, Hitachi, Ltd., Japan), whose acceleration voltage was 20 kV. And the element analysis was carried out by an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS, Apollo XLT SDD, EDAX, USA). Pictures of microstructures were taken by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-210, JEOL, Japan) at the operating voltage of 200 kV. Chemical compositions and elements were explored and analyzed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Al Kα, Thermo Fisher Scientific Company, US). We recorded the infrared spectra using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR650, PerkinElmer, USA) and the scanning was in the range of 550∼4000 cm–1. Quantitative analysis of HMIs was performed by an atomic adsorption spectrometer (PerkinElmer PinAAcle 900 T, United states).

A thorough and organized investigation was conducted on the interactions, motivating factors among CDs, Mg(OH)2, and cellulose in a ternary composite material Carbon Dots-Mg(OH)2
Cellulose (CMCel), with both experimental and theoretical approaches. Atomic-scale binary composites including Carbon Dots-Mg(OH)2 (CM_n, n = 1∼3), Mg(OH)2
Cellulose (MCel_n, n = 1∼4) and Carbon Dots-Cellulose (CCel_n, n = 1∼3) were studied by density functional theory (DFT). The interlayer structure of CMCel ternary composites was determined by formation reaction energy firstly. Subsequently, the geometric structures related to the adsorption and removal of Pb(II) from CMCel were thoroughly optimized. In Table S1 of Supporting Information, the studied composites, complexes and their abbreviations are presented.

The comprehensive optimization of all geometric structures of the composite, complexes and intermediates in gas phase was carried out using Priroda program [39–41]. The symmetry was not considered during the structural optimization. The convergence criteria for geometry optimization was set to 10−5 au, while self-consistent field convergence criterion for frequency calculations to 10−6 au. All-electron (AE) four-component relativistic Hamiltonian was used at scalar relativistic level for the calculation. Generalized gradient approximation (GGA) PBE functional [42–44] was applied with Gaussian basis set consistent with the all-electron energy. Frequency calculation was performed at the optimized geometry structure; and no imaginary frequencies were found, thus confirming the most stable structure.

To assess thermodynamic stability of composite, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were conducted using the DFTB+ [45] program. The simulations employed a NVT ensemble, where the Nosé-Hoover chain thermostat regulated the system temperature at 298.15 K. The GFN2 xTB [46] Hamiltonian was employed in the calculations. Simulation time step was set at 1 fs with a 10 ps in total.

To investigate the electronic structural properties, single-point calculations were conducted using the ADF program [47–50]. The optimized structure provided by Priroda was used, and the COSMO model was applied with water as the solvent, having a dielectric constant of 78.38. The Klamt radii were utilized for O, C, H and Mg, which were 1.72, 2.00, 1.30, and 1.73 Å, respectively. All ADF calculations employed the scalar relativistic ZORA Hamiltonian, Slater-type TZP basis sets and GGA-PBE functional. Moreover, when implementing the ZORA-TZP basis sets, a small core frozen-core treatment was applied by freezing the 1 s core orbitals of C, O and Mg. The SCF convergence criterion for all calculations was set to 10−6 au., and the integration grid parameter was set to 6.0 × 6.0 × 6.0.

Apart from calculated formation reaction energies, an energy decomposition analysis (EDA) in the ADF code was conducted on the composite and its related complexes to explore the interaction between the interfaces. To accurately portray weak interactions involved between composites interfaces, refinements were made to accurately describe weak interactions involving composite material interfaces through the use of Grimme's D3 version for dispersive correction [51,52]. The decomposition of the total bonding energy (ΔET) is as follows:
(1)
Δ
E T
=
E E
+
E P
+
E O
+
E D
(2)
Δ
E T
=
E S
+
E O
+
E D
ΔET is composed of various components, including electrostatic interaction (EE), Pauli repulsion (EP), orbital attraction (EO), Steric interaction (ES), and Dispersion interaction (ED); and EE and EP constitute ES.

To quantitatively investigate the bonding properties between interfaces, we employed the Gaussian 09 program [53] for single-point calculations, and carried out QTAIM (Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecule) topological analyses. Initially, we used the GGA-PBE functional and basis sets of 6–31G* for Mg, C, H and O atoms. The QTAIM analysis was performed using the Multiwfn 3.4.1 software [54], which allowed us to obtain the chemical-bond parameters at the interfacial bond critical points such as electron density ρ(r), Laplacian density ∇2ρ(r), and energy density H(r). All of them have unit of au.. H(r) was broken down into potential energy density V(r) and kinetic energy density G(r). Using an empirical formula of Eint = 0.5*V(r), we calculated the bond interaction energy Eint (eV).

SEM and TEM techniques would provide microstructures of CMCel as well as sense interactions of its components. The SEM images (Fig. 1c and 1d) indicate that the cellulose was covered with Mg(OH)2 flakes, resulting in a layer-by-layer general structure. The size of Mg(OH)2 flakes ranges from 30 to 50 nm. The TEM images taken in Fig. 1e and 1f also prove the layer structure for the composite. The Mg(OH)2 flakes are found very thin, and measured at only about 5 nm. The lattice fringes of 0.236 nm correspond to the interplanar spacing of the (101) plane of Mg(OH)2.

After conducting experimental synthesis and structural characterizations, DFT calculations were utilized to specify structures and properties of the CMCel composite at the atomic level. Firstly, the exact geometric structure of ternary CMCel was determined by constructing possible binary composites, including CM, MCel and CCel. The potentially stable structures of each binary composite were separately optimized, and their corresponding isomeric structures were illustrated in Figs. S1–S3. During the optimization process, three isomers of CM_n (n = 1∼3) that are energetically favorable and structurally stable were obtained. After analyzing the thermodynamic parameters (Table S2), it is discovered that CM_1 has the lowest energy and the most stable structure out of the three. Similarly, four isomers of MCel_n (n = 1∼4) were obtained, all of which were structurally stable. Moreover, three structurally stable isomers of CCel_n (n = 1∼3) were also obtained. To provide more intuitive picture from the thermodynamics, various formation reaction energies of the most stable isomer of each binary composite were plotted in Fig. 2 a. Upon analyzing the data, all of the built binary composites are found to be exothermic; and the order of energy (absolute value) is MCel_1 > CM_1 > CCel_1. It indicates that the binary composites formed by Mg(OH)2 with CDs and Cel are more thermodynamically preferred than those formed by CDs and Cel. Thus, it is determined that the geometric structure of the ternary composites would have Mg(OH)2 located between carbon dots and cellulose. This agrees with electron microscopy pictures in Fig. 1.

Furthermore, the geometric structure of the ternary composite CMCel was fully optimized. Four most stable isomers, CMCel_n (n = 1∼4), were depicted in Fig. 3 . As expected, all these isomers are thermodynamically favorable. Their formation is spontaneous because of calculated free energies in range from −1.107 to −0.679 eV (Fig. 2b and Table S2). Notably, CMCel_1 has the lowest energy with the most stable structure, whose reaction energy is nearly the sum of those of the binary composites CM_1 and MCel_1. This is consistent with the generation of two interfaces in the ternary composite.

Notably, the good thermal and structural stability of CMCel has been proven by the MD simulations, in line with its experimental accessibility. From Fig. S4, one can see that the structure of the ternary composite remained intact at 298.15 K, even experiencing 10 ps geometry relaxation. It can be attributed to the strong chemical interfacial coupling. Our results agree with previous reports [3,56]. Similarly, their MD simulations corroborated the stability of N- and S-modified titanium carbide MXenes, offering the possibility of experimental synthesis of the 2D materials.

The ternary composite CMCel could be stabilized by attaining energy from formation of two interfaces CDs/MH and MH/Cel. Their strength and nature were investigated by EDA in terms of the four isomers CMCel_n (n = 1∼4). Further comparison was made with binary composites CM, MCel and CCel (Table S3). To facilitate comparison, these EDA data of CMCel are also presented graphically in Fig. 4 . The computed ET energy at the CDs/MH interface is located between −1.963 and −1.931 eV, whereas the one at the MH/Cel interface ranges from −2.492 to −1.135 eV. These values are quite close to −1.940 eV of CM_1 and −2.469 eV of MCel_1, respectively. The addition of one more component has a small effect on formation of the ternary composite, suggesting the stability of the latter.

The energetically lowest-energy isomer CMCel_1 would be the most prevalent appearing local structure in the composite. Its computed ET at the CDs/MH interface is −1.931 eV, which comprises 0.547 eV ES, −2.057 eV EO and −0.421 eV ED. The steric term is differentiated into EE of −2.641 eV and EP of 3.188 eV, whereby the former is relatively smaller in absolute magnitude. Generally, EO, EE and ED are considered as covalent, ionic and weak interactions, respectively. It is apparent that the main interaction at the interface is ionic interaction (51.59 %), with orbital attraction accounting for 40.18 %. It indicates that the electrostatic attraction at CDs/MH is principal for forming stable structure. It is worth noting that the computed ED is −0.421 eV, holding 8.23 %. The dispersion corresponds to weak interactions of vdW. Taking account of hydroxyls in each component present at the interface, hydrogen bonds would exist too. Precise explanation will be provided through QTAIM topological analyses. Possessing a pure CDs/MH interface, the binary composite material CM_1 exhibits nearly the same ET at −1.940 eV, which is also governed by electrostatic attraction with smaller orbital and dispersion interactions.

The MH/Cel interface of CMCel_1 has a more negative ET (−2.492 eV) value than the CDs/MH interface. This is due to the increase in electrostatic interaction that plays a larger role (55.29 %), while the orbital attraction is reduced to 39.85 %. This can be attributed to the decreased ED from the CDs/MH interface to the MH/Cel interface, which is −0.360 eV and accounts for 4.86 %. This trend is also evident in the binary composites CM_1 and MCel_1, where their respective ED value is found to be −0.421 eV (8.23 %) and −0.36 eV (4.87 %). Thus, it indicates that hydrogen bonds are more pronounced at the CDs/MH's interface.

From a chemical bonding perspective, QTAIM calculations can provide a quantitative topological analysis on the interfaces in the ternary composite CMCel_1. Various parameters were obtained at interfacial BCPs. Typically, a strong hydrogen bonding interaction is considered to exist when 0.04 < ρ(r) < 0.12 and H(r) < 0. On the other hand, when ρ(r) > 0, H(r) < 0 and ∇2ρ(r) > 0, the bonding interaction at interfaces indicates a weak hydrogen bond. The calculated results reveal that there is a strong hydrogen bonding interaction at the CDs/MH interface, as demonstrated by the maximum electron density of the HC∙∙∙OM bond, which is 0.084 (Table 1 ), within the above range. The electron densities of the other three OC∙∙∙HM bonds are between 0.027 and 0.038, indicating weak hydrogen bonds. As for the MH/Cel interface, the HM∙∙∙O bond has a maximum electron density of 0.078, weaker than that of the CDs/MH interfaces, which is consistent with the EDA results. By comparing the QTAIM parameters of the binary composites CM_1 and MH_1 separately (Table S4), it is found that the same regularity applies.

Finally, a detailed examination was carried out on the electronic behavior of CMCel_1. Figs. 5 and S4, along with the orbital composition analysis presented in Table S5, reveal that the CDs moiety solely contributes to the H-1 ∼ L+4 and L+6 orbitals in the ternary composite material, indicating that CDs can regulate the stability and selectivity of adsorbates. Furthermore, the collaborative electronic impact of Mg(OH)2 and cellulose is evident in the L+9 (41.45 % and 33.93 %), H-4 (57.47 % and 16.88 %), and H-9 (41.45 % and 33.93 %) orbitals. These orbitals, to some degree, indicate strong interfacial interactions between Mg(OH)2 and cellulose, and provide excellent interfacial coupling. This is consistent with a large interaction energy (the sum of Eint: Eint(T) of −2.826 eV from QTAIM and ET of −2.492 eV from EDA) and corroborates the formation of stable ternary composite material. H-2 is contributed by the 2p orbitals of hydroxyl oxygen in both Mg(OH)2 and CDs. H-3 is contributed by Mg(OH)2, CDs, and Cel collectively. H-4 is contributed by Mg(OH)2 and Cel. Overall, in H-2, H-3, and H-4 (Fig. S5), the contribution of Mg(OH)2 with CDs is greater than that of Cel. This trend is consistent with the hydrogen bonding strength between Mg(OH)2 and CDs being stronger than that between Mg(OH)2 and Cel, as observed in the QTAIM and EDA analyses. The high-energy unoccupied orbitals and low-energy occupied orbitals were primarily contributed by CDs, Mg(OH)2, and cellulose, such as L+10 ∼ L+12, L+15 ∼ L+20, and H-14 ∼ H-17. Furthermore, a comparison was made between the density of states of binary and ternary composites (Fig. S6), indirectly verifying the electronic properties between the interfaces of the ternary composite. In the CMCel_1 ternary composite, the low-energy unoccupied and high-energy occupied orbitals are solely contributed by the CM_1 portion, mainly derived from CDs. However, the high-energy unoccupied and low-energy occupied orbitals are mainly of both CM_1 and MCel_1, with cellulose playing the dominant part. Overall, the interface interaction effectively couples these components, resulting in the formation of a stable ternary composite material.

The ternary composite CMCel has been utilized to capture and immobilize HMI Pb(II) from aqueous solution. XRD was used to characterize the resultant material firstly. The patterns in Fig. 6 a reveal that the recovered material exhibits the phase of Pb3(CO3)2(OH)2 (JCPDS number: 13–0131). The sharp peaks indicate a high crystallinity of lead basic carbonate. Characteristic diffraction peaks of cellulose are also noticeable. No other peaks are observed, providing evidence that Mg(OH)2 has completely transformed into carbonate salt. SEM images in Fig. 6b and 6c depict the morphology of the recovered material. One can note that the hexagon-like slices of Mg(OH)2 are turned into triangular slices of PbCO3 upon up-taking Pb(II). EDS mappings in Fig. 6d demonstrate that Pb(II) held by the composite is evenly distributed on cellulose.

To explore the adsorption process, we conducted a detailed XPS analysis of the sample that CMCel adsorbed Pb2+. In Fig. 6e, peaks of related to elements Pb, O and C were observed, indicating their existence in the recovered sample. Notably, the ones are located at binding energies of 138.7 and 143.5 eV (Fig. 6f), which are attributed to Pb 4f7/2 and Pb 4f5/2, respectively. This suggests that lead has been recovered from the water and deposited onto the cellulose substrate. Careful inspection on XPS does not find signals belonging to Mg. So an exchange mechanism of Mg2+ with Pb2+ is proposed for the lead removal. Taken together, the recovered sample is characterized to have the composition of cellulose-Pb3(CO3)2(OH)2.

The CMCel composite is capable of removing heavy metal Pb(II) ion quickly and completely according to adsorption experiment. The results, depicted in Fig. 7 a, indicate that the composite has achieved adsorption equilibrium within 24 h, with an impressive adsorption capacity of 1623.0 mg g–1. The linear fitting based on the pseudo-first and second-order kinetics (Fig. 7b) gives the R2 values of 0.7997 and 0.8146, respectively. The latter equation is better to describe the removal process. From it, the q e value was calculated to be 1657.5 mg g–1, which is comparable to the experimental value. Furthermore, our composite works out Pb2+ ion concentrations ranging from 0 to 2000 mg L–1 (Fig. 7c); and the equilibrium concentration is at 500 mg L–1. The modellings show the R2 value of Langmuir model (0.9883) is closer to linearity than the one of Freundlich model (Fig. 7d and 7e). It suggests that the uptake of Pb(II) follows a monolayer adsorption. The sample shows good durability for it works even after 10 cycles in treating lead wastewater. As depicted in Fig. 7f, the removal efficiency retains above 99.5 % after the 10th-cycle operation.

Cations may affect the adsorption of Pb2+. So we carried out experiments of interfering ions such as K+, Ca2+, Na+, and Mg2+. As depicted in Fig. 8 a, the removal efficiency of Pb2+ constantly remains 100 %. Moreover, it is applicable in a very large range of interfering cation concentrations from 100 to 800 mg L–1. These results indicate that the presence of surrounding cations has a negligible effect on the HMI removal by CMCel.

The pH value of solution is believed as a factor that significantly impacts the adsorption performance. A systematic study was conducted on solutions with pH values of 3, 5, 7 and 9. The results in Fig. 8b show a pronounced increase in the adsorption capacity of CMCel to Pb(II), as the pH value rises from 3 to 9. Specifically, the removal rate is only 40 % at pH = 3, which is attributed to the dissolution of magnesium hydroxide. It comes from a reduction of the effective component in the composite. At pH levels of 7 or 9, the removal efficiency maintains higher than that of the blank test.

To supplement experimental findings and clarify the mechanism behind the adsorbent's ability to remove heavy metal Pb(II), DFT calculations were performed on complexed intermediate and recovered product.

Associated the experiment with DFT calculations, the ternary composite CMCel readily captures lead nitrate via the electrostatic attraction. It is intuitively bore out by the electrostatic potential in Fig. 9 . Then the intermediate CMCel·PbNO3 , which is thermodynamically stable, is spontaneously formed because the process has negative reaction free energies. After transformation reaction, the lead basic carbonate is chemically immobilized by the substrate cellulose. In brief, the lead ions can be quickly converted into insoluble solid salts and tightly held by the cellulose, which accomplishes HMI removal from wastewater effectively and completely.

Electron-microscopy and spectroscopic characterizations reveal a general layered structure for composite CMCel. Therein, the cellulose is covered with Mg(OH)2 flakes that are 30∼50 nm long/wide and 5 nm thick; and the (101) plane of Mg(OH)2 is identified by the 0.236 nm interplanar spacing. The local interfacial structures are recognized by DFT calculations, where components of CDs and Mg(OH)2 are self-assembled onto the cellulose substrate in sequence. It is found that the interactions between the interfaces of the composite material are primarily electrostatic in nature, with chemical coupling of hydrogen bonds.

CMCel is able to remove toxic divalent lead ions rapidly and completely. It reaches equilibrium within 30 min with an adsorption capacity as high as 1623.0 mg g–1, which follows a pseudo-second-order kinetic mechanism. Excellent durability is acquired in treating wastewater, for the composite maintains a removal rate of above 99.5 % even after 10-cycle operation.

The immobilization of HMI by CMCel is further realized by a combination of experimental exploration and DFT calculations. The composite starts capturing lead ion by the electrostatic driving. Then the intermediate, being thermodynamically stable is produced with the release of 0.650 eV free energy. Ultimately, the precipitate is gathered by cellulose. The whole process is spontaneous and exothermic.

In summary, experimentally and theoretically suggested pseudo-dynamic adsorption mechanism has helped understand the HMI removal by biomass composite. The involved interfacial interactions are elaborated on. This study is expected to provide a basis of designing and developing novel renewable adsorbents for wastewater treatment.
Supplementary material pertaining to the article can be found in the online version.

Hong-Xue Cai: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Chen Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wen-Xiu Zheng: Investigation. Yuan-Ru Guo: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Qing-Jiang Pan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (22276046 and U2167219); and the fund of the reformation and development of local universities supported by Central Government (2021) is appreciated. QJP is grateful to Dr. Dimitri Laikov for providing us with the Priroda code.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.104466.
